agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals." },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of customer request volume relative to available delivery capacity in a given timeframe and/or geography. Exact definition unspecified." },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds or constraints that prevent prices from reaching extreme or unreasonable levels." },
      { "term": "Delivery Price", "definition": "The fee charged to a customer for a delivery, which DynaPricing will adjust dynamically." },
      { "term": "Real Time", "definition": "Price adjustments occur continuously or near-continuously in response to demand changes, not on a batch/manual schedule." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to normal levels.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. The direction and magnitude of adjustment is a business decision that leadership could change (e.g., cap surge pricing, adopt flat pricing for loyalty customers). It does not corrupt system state if violated.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'high', the computed delivery price MUST be greater than the base delivery price."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to normal levels.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy to drive volume during low-demand periods. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead. No data corruption if violated.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'low', the computed delivery price MUST be less than the base delivery price."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the system must react to demand changes as they happen, not on a delayed or manual schedule.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational requirement about timeliness. The business could decide to batch-update prices every 5 minutes instead of per-request. No state corruption if delayed.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand level changes, the new price must be reflected in subsequent price quotes within an acceptable latency window (threshold TBD)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no manual human intervention required for price adjustments during normal operation.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy. The business could introduce manual override or approval steps. No state corruption if a human intervenes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments must be computed and applied without requiring human approval in the standard flow."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values — there must be an upper bound (price ceiling) and a lower bound (price floor).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without bounds, an automated system could produce absurd prices (e.g., $0.00 or $10,000 for a local delivery), causing financial loss, regulatory exposure, or reputational damage. A price that violates physical or financial reality represents corrupt system output. The existence of bounds is an invariant; the specific values of those bounds are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level, the computed delivery price MUST satisfy: price_floor <= computed_price <= price_ceiling, where both bounds are defined and price_floor > 0."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical for a courier service. It would mean paying customers to take deliveries. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The computed delivery price MUST be > 0 for every price calculation, regardless of demand level or any discount applied."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing model must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices should not be so high that customers leave, nor so low that deliveries are unprofitable.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The thresholds for 'competitive' and 'profitable' are policy decisions that will evolve. A VP could choose to run at a loss to gain market share.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price floor must be >= the marginal cost of delivery (profitability). The price ceiling must be <= a competitive market threshold (competitiveness). Exact values TBD by business."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Demand must be quantifiable — the system must have a defined, measurable signal or metric that classifies current demand level.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If demand cannot be measured, the entire pricing function has no input and produces arbitrary output. The system's core premise is demand-driven pricing; without a demand signal, output is not meaningful — it is corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price calculation must reference a current demand measurement. A price quote must never be generated without an associated demand signal."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or the demand measurement system is down.", "expected_behavior": "The system must fall back to a safe default price (e.g., base price) rather than failing to quote or producing an unbounded price. This fallback behavior is unspecified by the CEO." },
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., major event, emergency, weather disaster).", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails (price ceiling) must cap the price. But should there be rate-of-change limits (e.g., price can't jump more than 3x in 5 minutes)? Unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to near-zero.", "expected_behavior": "Price floor must prevent price from dropping below cost. But should there be a minimum margin? Unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws during emergencies.", "expected_behavior": "In many jurisdictions, surge pricing during declared emergencies is illegal. The CEO's description does not address regulatory compliance. This is a significant gap." },
    { "scenario": "Concurrent rapid oscillation in demand (demand flapping).", "expected_behavior": "Prices could oscillate wildly between high and low, confusing customers. Should there be smoothing, hysteresis, or minimum hold periods? Unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, confirms order, but demand changed between quote and confirmation.", "expected_behavior": "Should the quoted price be honored, or should the new price apply? Price-locking policy is unspecified. This has major customer trust implications." },
    { "scenario": "Geographic variation — demand is high in one zone, low in another.", "expected_behavior": "Should pricing be zone-specific? The CEO says 'demand' without specifying granularity. Unspecified." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin on every delivery. Correct guardrails protect against revenue loss (floor too low) and demand destruction (ceiling too high). Incorrect implementation could cause immediate, per-transaction financial damage at scale.",
    "operational": "Full automation removes manual pricing overhead but introduces dependency on demand signal reliability and system uptime. Pricing system becomes a critical-path dependency for order flow.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility and perceived unfairness (e.g., surge pricing during emergencies) are major UX and brand risks.",
    "risk": "High risk from regulatory exposure (price-gouging laws), reputational risk from perceived unfairness, and financial risk from guardrail misconfiguration. Fully automated systems without human circuit-breakers can cause damage at machine speed.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Direct price variability on every order. Trust and fairness perception. Price transparency expectations." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Dynamic pricing may affect their compensation if pay is tied to delivery fees. High-demand periods may mean more work and/or higher pay." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes more complex with variable pricing. Margin analysis requires real-time data." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must ensure compliance with price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and fair pricing requirements across jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Pricing changes affect order volume patterns, which in turn affect capacity planning and courier allocation." },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers transparently. Brand risk from perceived unfairness." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. It establishes directional intent (demand-based, automated, guardrailed) but leaves virtually every concrete parameter undefined: demand measurement method, price bounds, adjustment algorithm, geographic granularity, temporal granularity, fallback behavior, price-locking policy, regulatory compliance, rate-of-change limits, and competitive/profitability thresholds. The word 'probably' before guardrails is especially concerning — it signals even the guardrails aren't committed to. This is insufficient to build against.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand is high (directional intent is clear)",
      "Prices go down when demand is low (directional intent is clear)",
      "System should be automated",
      "Some form of guardrails is desired"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined",
      "What the guardrail values are (price floor, price ceiling) — 'probably' qualifier",
      "Whether guardrails include rate-of-change limits or just absolute bounds",
      "Geographic and temporal granularity of demand measurement",
      "Price-locking policy between quote and order confirmation",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements across jurisdictions",
      "Fallback behavior when demand signal is unavailable",
      "How 'competitive' and 'profitable' are quantified",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing",
      "Whether customers will see price justification or just final price"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "There must be a base/reference price from which adjustments are made (not stated but implied by 'adjust')",
      "Demand is measured as request volume relative to capacity (not explicitly defined)",
      "Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor (CEO only said 'prices don't go crazy' — implies ceiling, floor is assumed)",
      "Price must remain positive (physical/financial constraint, not stated)",
      "The system operates per-delivery, not as aggregate/subscription pricing"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO used the word 'probably' regarding guardrails ('We'll probably have some guardrails'). This means even the existence of price bounds is not confirmed, let alone their values. Guardrails are critical for an automated pricing system — without them, the system can produce harmful prices at machine speed.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["A) Treat guardrails as mandatory and define default bounds", "B) Make guardrails optional/configurable", "C) Escalate to CEO for explicit commitment"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — guardrails must be mandatory for any automated pricing system. Escalate to CEO for explicit price floor and ceiling values."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of demand measurement: What metric represents demand? Order volume per hour? Orders per courier? Geographic density? Without this, the core input to the pricing function is undefined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Marketing", "Operations/Dispatch", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Define demand as orders-per-hour vs available-couriers ratio", "B) Use multiple demand signals with weighted scoring", "C) Request product team to define demand model"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — demand measurement is a core domain concept that must be explicitly defined before implementation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No price-locking policy defined. If a customer sees a quoted price and demand changes before order confirmation, which price applies? This directly affects customer trust and revenue.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["A) Lock quoted price for a time window (e.g., 5 minutes)", "B) Always use current price at confirmation time", "C) Honor quoted price if lower, use current if lower (customer-favorable)"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — lock the quoted price for a reasonable window. This is industry standard for ride-sharing and delivery surge pricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have price-gouging laws that prohibit surge pricing during declared emergencies. An automated system that ignores this creates legal liability.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Build emergency override that caps prices during declared emergencies", "B) Exclude certain jurisdictions from dynamic pricing", "C) Engage legal team before implementation"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — legal review must happen before any dynamic pricing system ships. Option A should be built regardless as a safeguard."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No definition of geographic or temporal granularity. Is demand measured city-wide, per-zone, per-neighborhood? Per-minute, per-hour, per-day? This fundamentally changes the system's behavior and complexity.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/Dispatch", "Customers", "Product/Marketing"],
      "options": ["A) Zone-based with hourly windows", "B) Point-based with real-time continuous measurement", "C) Request product team to define granularity model"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — granularity is a core design decision with major operational and customer experience implications. Must be explicitly defined."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
